title: Cantar de mio Cid
titleEn: The Song of the Cid — The Exile Begins (tiradas 1–4, lines 1–64)
source: Ramón Menéndez Pidal (ed.), Poema de mio Cid, 2.ª edición corregida (Clásicos Castellanos 24; Madrid: Ediciones de “La Lectura”, 1923), pp. 122–128, lines 1–64 (tiradas 1–4). Text follows Pidal's critical text exactly, including its old accentuation and mixed spellings (puorta/puerta, llegaua/osava); [brackets] mark letters and words he prints in italics as editorial restorations; the edition's line-initial repeated quotation marks are reduced to one opening and one closing mark per speech. Line numbers are Pidal's — the standard citation system, with his 14b/16b kept so the traditional count is undisturbed. Pronunciation convention: reconstructed twelfth-century Castilian (ç=ts, z=dz, x=sh, j=zh, ss/s distinct, initial f- and v both sounded). Translations made fresh from the original.
about: The Cantar de mio Cid survives in a single manuscript, the Vivar codex, whose colophon names the copyist Per Abbat and the year 1207 (the surviving codex may itself be a fourteenth-century copy). Its first leaf is lost: the poem begins mid-scene, on the most famous tear in Spanish literature. What came before survives only in prose retellings in later chronicles, and everything here is an edition — Menéndez Pidal's of 1923 — whose [bracketed] letters mark his restorations, some of them (like the whole of verse 14b) taken from those chronicles. The notes say so where it matters. Rodrigo Díaz of Vivar, 'mio Çid' (Arabic for 'my lord'), was a real Castilian noble who died in Valencia in 1099; the poem, composed within about a century of his death, opens as King Alfonso VI sends him into exile on the strength of his enemies' accusations.

sentences.t:
De los sos ojos tan fuertemientre llorando,
tornava la cabeça i estávalos catando.
sentences.l:
From his eyes so mightily weeping,
he turned the head and was beholding them.
sentences.i: Weeping hard from his eyes, he turned his head and gazed back at them.
sentences.n: Tirada 1 (assonance á-o) — the Cid looks back at his emptied house at Vivar. The poem begins mid-scene: the manuscript's first leaf is lost, so this famous tear-filled line is literally the first that survives. sos = sus 'his'; catar = 'to look at' (archaic; modern mirar); i = 'and' (usually e).

sentences.t:
Vío puertas abiertas e uços sin cañados,
alcándaras vázias sin pielles e sin mantos
e sin falcones e sin adtores mudados.
sentences.l:
He-saw doors open and house-doors without padlocks,
perches empty without pelts and without mantles
and without falcons and without hawks moulted.
sentences.i: He saw gates standing open and doors without their locks, the perches empty of fur cloaks and mantles, of falcons and of moulted goshawks.
sentences.n: An inventory of stripped wealth: uços (Latin ostium) are the inner doors, cañados = candados 'padlocks'; the alcándaras are the racks where furs hung and hunting-birds perched. adtores = azores 'goshawks'; a hawk that has moulted (mudado) in captivity is a trained, valuable bird.

sentences.t: Sospiró mio Çid, ca mucho avié grandes cuidados.
sentences.l: Sighed my Çid, for greatly he-had great cares.
sentences.i: My Cid sighed, for heavy cares weighed upon him.
sentences.n: mio Çid — 'my lord', from Arabic sīdī: the hero's standing epithet (Ç = ts, so 'Tsid'). ca = 'for, because'. avié = 'had' (imperfect of aver, modern haber — aver as a full verb 'to have, hold' is everywhere in this poem).

sentences.t:
Fabló mio Çid bien e tan mesurado:
“grado a tí, señor padre, que estás en alto!
Esto me an buolto mios enemigos malos.”
sentences.l:
Spoke my Çid well and so measured:
“thanks to thee, lord father, who art on high!
This to-me have stirred-up my enemies wicked.”
sentences.i: My Cid spoke, well and with great measure: “Thanks be to thee, Lord Father on high! This is the brew my wicked enemies have stirred for me.”
sentences.n: fablar = hablar — Old Spanish keeps Latin f- where modern Spanish has h- (fabló, fecha, fincar, falcón): you'll see this constantly. mesurado: mesura, measured self-command, is THE courtly virtue of this poem. buolto (past part. of bolver, modern volver) = 'stirred up, brewed' — bolver traición was 'to weave treachery': the Cid was exiled on false accusations before the king.

sentences.t: Allí pienssan de aguijar, allí sueltan las riendas.
sentences.l: There they-think to spur, there they-loosen the reins.
sentences.i: Then they set spurs to their horses; then they slacken the reins.
sentences.n: Tirada 2 (assonance ié-a) — omens on the road to Burgos. A change of assonance (the vowel-pattern shared by the line-endings) marks a new tirada, the poem's paragraph. pensar de + infinitive = 'to set about doing' — a stock epic formula.

sentences.t:
A la exida de Bivar, ovieron la corneja diestra,
e entrando a Burgos oviéronla siniestra.
sentences.l:
At the going-out from Bivar, they-had the crow on-the-right,
and entering at Burgos they-had-it on-the-left.
sentences.i: As they rode out of Vivar the crow was on their right; as they entered Burgos, it was on their left.
sentences.n: Bird augury: right = favorable, left = ill-omened. Leaving home the signs are good; at Burgos they turn bad. exida = salida (Latin exire 'go out'). ovieron, oviéronla — aver again, with the object pronoun glued on the end (oviéron+la).

sentences.t:
Meçió mio Çid los ombros y engrameó la tiesta:
“albricia, Alvar Fáñez, ca echados somos de tierra!
mas a grand ondra tornaremos a Castiella.”
sentences.l:
Shook my Çid the shoulders and tossed the head:
“good-tidings, Alvar Fáñez, for cast-out are we from land!
but to great honor we-shall-return to Castile.”
sentences.i: My Cid shrugged his shoulders and tossed his head: “Good tidings, Álvar Fáñez — we are banished from the land! But to great honor we shall return to Castile.”
sentences.n: The shrug shakes off the bad omen. albricia — a cry for good news (Arabic al-bishāra), defiant irony here. Álvar Fáñez is the Cid's kinsman and right hand. The last line is missing in the manuscript: Pidal restores it (as verse 14b, printed in italics in his edition) from the prose chronicles that preserve a fuller text of the poem. ondra = honra.

sentences.t:
Mio Çid [Roy] Díaz por Burgos entróv[e],
En su[e] conpaña sessaenta pendones;
sentences.l:
My Çid Roy Díaz through Burgos entered,
In his company sixty pennons;
sentences.i: My Cid Ruy Díaz rode into Burgos, sixty pennons in his company.
sentences.n: Tirada 3 (assonance ó-e) — the Cid enters Burgos. Letters in [brackets] are Pidal's restorations, printed as italics in his edition. Here the copyist wrote entrava, spoiling the assonance; the restored entróve carries the archaic paragogic -e, an extra final -e sung in epic verse — it marks every rhyme-word of this tirada (sone, dolore, razóne, señore). Sixty pendones: pennons fly from lances, so men are counted by them. Roy = Ruy, short form of Rodrigo.

sentences.t:
exien lo ve[e]r mugieres e varones,
burgeses e burgesas, por las finiestras son[e],
plorando de los ojos, tanto avien el dolor[e].
sentences.l:
went-out him to-see women and men,
burghers and burgheresses, at the windows are,
weeping from the eyes, so-much they-had the grief.
sentences.i: Men and women came out to see him; the burghers and their wives are at the windows, weeping from their eyes, so great was their grief.
sentences.n: This extra line is numbered 16b so as not to disturb the poem's traditional line-count (the citation numbers were fixed before Pidal's edition). finiestras = ventanas (Latin fenestra). plorando — the very word that opened the poem as llorando, here in its older pl- form: the language is caught mid-change, and the poet uses both. exien = salían.

sentences.t:
De las sus bocas todos dizían una razón[e]:
“Dios, qué buen vassallo, si oviesse buen señor[e]!”
sentences.l:
From the their mouths all were-saying one saying:
“God, what-a good vassal, if he-had a good lord!”
sentences.i: From every mouth came the same word: “God, what a good vassal — if only he had a good lord!”
sentences.n: The most famous line of the poem: Burgos laments that so loyal a vassal is wasted on so ungrateful a king. si + imperfect subjunctive (oviesse) with no main clause is a pure wish: 'if only…'. las sus bocas — article + possessive together is normal Old Spanish.

sentences.t:
Conbidar le ien de grado, mas ninguno non osava:
el rey don Alfonsso tanto avie le grand saña.
sentences.l:
To-invite him they-would with pleasure, but no-one not dared:
the king don Alfonsso so-much had toward-him great wrath.
sentences.i: They would gladly have taken him in, but no one dared: so great was King Alfonso's wrath against him.
sentences.n: Tirada 4 (assonance á-a) — no one shelters the Cid; only a nine-year-old girl speaks to him, and he must camp on the river gravel outside Burgos. Conbidar le ien = le convidarían — the Old Spanish conditional is still separable: infinitive + ien (from ién < avién), with the pronoun tucked inside. Watch for this split again. ninguno non — double negative is standard grammar here, not emphasis.

sentences.t:
Antes de la noche en Burgos dél entró su carta,
con grand recabdo e fuertemientre se[e]llada:
sentences.l:
Before the night into Burgos from-him entered his letter,
with great strictness and strongly sealed:
sentences.i: Before nightfall his letter had entered Burgos, under strict order and firmly sealed:
sentences.n: dél = de él, 'from him' (the king). recabdo (modern recaudo) = care, strict precaution. seellada — from seello, Latin sigillum (modern sello); Pidal restores the older double e.

sentences.t:
que a mio Çid Roy Díaz que nadi nol diessen posada,
e aquel que gela diesse sopiesse vera palabra
que perderie los averes e más los ojos de la cara,
e aun demás los cuerpos e las almas.
sentences.l:
that to my Çid Roy Díaz that nobody not-to-him should-give lodging,
and that-one who to-him-it should-give should-know true word
that he-would-lose the goods and moreover the eyes of the face,
and even besides the bodies and the souls.
sentences.i: that no one was to give lodging to my Cid Ruy Díaz; and whoever did should know it for sure truth: he would lose his goods, and the very eyes from his face, and body and soul into the bargain.
sentences.n: The enclitic squeeze: nol = non le 'not to him'; gela = ge la 'it to him' (Old Spanish ge became modern se: se la). nadi = nadie. The penalty ladder — property, eyes, body, soul — echoes the curse-clauses of real royal charters; Alfonso VI used them in his own documents.

sentences.t:
Grande duelo avien las yentes cristianas;
ascóndense de mio Çid, ca nol osan dezir nada.
sentences.l:
Great grief had the folk Christian;
they-hide-themselves from my Çid, for not-him they-dare to-say nothing.
sentences.i: Great was the grief of the Christian folk; they hide from my Cid, for none dares say a word to him.
sentences.n: las yentes cristianas simply means 'everyone' (in negative phrases cristianos = 'a living soul'). yentes = gentes. Note the tense-switch avien (past) → ascóndense, osan (present): the epic narrator slides into the present for vividness — you'll see this constantly.

sentences.t:
El Campeador adeliñó a su posada;
así como llegó a la puorta, fallóla bien çerrada,
por miedo del rey Alfons, que assí lo para[ran]:
que si non la quebrantás, que non gela abriesse[n] por nad[a].
sentences.i: The Campeador rode straight to his own lodging; the moment he reached the door, he found it locked fast — for fear of King Alfonso they had so agreed: unless he broke it down, they would not open to him for anything.
sentences.n: El Campeador, 'the Battler' (campo, the field of battle) — the Cid's other standing epithet. puorta here but puerta three lines on: the copyist wavers between the older uo and newer ue diphthong, and the edition keeps each as written. fallóla = la halló (f- again, fallar = hallar). quebrantás = quebrantasse, apocopated subjunctive.

sentences.t:
Los de mio Çid a altas vozes llaman,
los de dentro non les querién tornar palabra.
sentences.l:
Those of my Çid at high voices call,
those of within not to-them would return word.
sentences.i: My Cid's men call out at the top of their voices; those within would not return them a word.
My Cid spurred up close to the door, slipped his foot from the stirrup and gave it a kick — the door does not open, for it was locked fast.
sentences.n: ferídal = ferida + l(e): 'a blow to it', the object pronoun fused onto the noun — the enclitic squeeze at its boldest (una ferídal dava = le daba una ferida). A kick without dismounting: gesture, not force — he will not break the king's ban. llegaua — the edition keeps the copyist's u-for-v spelling here (beside osava, dava).

sentences.t: Una niña de nuef años a ojo se parava:
sentences.l: A girl of nine years before-the-eye herself placed:
sentences.i: A little girl of nine years stood forth before him:
sentences.n: nuef = nueve. a ojo — 'in plain sight'. In all Burgos, the one creature who dares address the banished man is a child.

sentences.t:
“Ya Campeador, en buen[a] çinxiestes espada!
El rey lo ha vedado, anoch dél e[n]tró su carta,
con grant recabdo e fuertemientre se[e]llada.
sentences.l:
“O Battler, in a-good (hour) you-girded sword!
The king it has forbidden, last-night from-him entered his letter,
with great strictness and strongly sealed.
sentences.i: “O Campeador, in a good hour you girded on your sword! The king has forbidden it: last night his letter came, under strict order and firmly sealed.
sentences.n: Ya = 'O' (the vocative particle, Arabic yā). en buen[a] çinxiestes espada — the poem's great honorific formula, 'in a good hour you girded on your sword', with ora 'hour' left unspoken; çinxiestes is the strong preterite of çeñir 'to gird' (Latin cinxistis). anoch = anoche. The girl unknowingly repeats the letter's own words from line 24.

sentences.i: We should not dare open to you or take you in for anything; else we should lose our goods and our houses, and the very eyes from our faces.
sentences.n: coger here = acoger, 'take in, shelter'. The child recites the penalty formula almost word for word — the whole town has it by heart.

sentences.t:
Çid, en el nuestro mal vos non ganades nada;
mas el Criador vos vala con todas sus vertudes santas.”
sentences.l:
Çid, in the our harm you not gain nothing;
but the Creator you may-aid with all his powers holy.”
sentences.i: Cid, by our ruin you would gain nothing; but may the Creator keep you, with all his holy powers.”
sentences.n: vala — present subjunctive of valer, 'may he avail (protect) you'. A blessing to soften the shut door. el nuestro mal — article + possessive again.

sentences.t: Esto la niña dixo e tornós pora su casa.
sentences.l: This the girl said and turned-herself toward her house.
sentences.i: So the girl spoke, and turned back to her house.
sentences.n: tornós = tornóse — after a stressed final vowel the -se shrinks to -s (you'll meet Partiós below). pora = para. dixo = dijo, with x = the sh-sound [ʃ].

sentences.t: Ya lo vede el Çid que del rey non avie graçia.
sentences.l: Now it sees the Çid that from-the king not he-had grace.
sentences.i: Now the Cid sees it plain: from the king there is no grace for him.
sentences.n: ve[d]e — the manuscript reads vee; Pidal restores the older vede (Latin videt). graçia = favor, good will.

sentences.i: He turned from the door and spurred through Burgos; coming to Santa María he dismounts at once, went down on his knees, and prayed from his heart.
sentences.n: Santa María is the cathedral of Burgos. fincar los inojos, 'plant the knees' = kneel (inojos = hinojos). dela and aguijaua — the copyist's run-together spelling and u-for-v, kept as printed. Again the present (descavalga) inside the past.

sentences.t:
La oraçión fecha, luego cavalgava;
salió por la puerta e Arlançón pa[ss]ava.
sentences.l:
The prayer made, straightway he-rode;
he-went-out through the gate and Arlançón he-was-crossing.
sentences.i: His prayer done, he rode on at once, out through the gate and over the Arlanzón.
sentences.n: la oraçión fecha — 'the prayer (being) made', a Latin-style absolute phrase; fecha = hecha (f- again). puerta here is the city gate. The Arlanzón is the river of Burgos: the Cid is now outside the walls.

sentences.t:
Cabo [Burgos] essa villa en la glera posava,
fincava la tienda e luego descavalgava.
sentences.l:
Beside Burgos that town on the gravel-bank he-camped,
he-planted the tent and straightway dismounted.
sentences.i: Beside the town of Burgos he made camp on the river-shingle; he pitched his tent and dismounted.
sentences.n: The glera is the bare gravel flat of the river-bed — the exile must camp on the shingle like a man outside the law. cabo = 'beside' (cf. modern cabe). fincar again: it 'plants' knees, tents, whatever is set firm.

sentences.i: My Cid Ruy Díaz, he who girded on his sword in a good hour, camped on the shingle, since no one would take him into a house — and around him, a good company of men.
sentences.n: çinxo — the same strong preterite as çinxiestes, now third person: the narrator takes up the girl's formula. Repetition with variation is how the epic builds its refrains. nol coge nadi — double negative, present tense, enclitic: three flagged patterns in four words.

sentences.t: Assí posó mio Çid commo si f[o]sse en montaña.
sentences.l: Thus camped my Çid as if he-were in wilderness.
sentences.i: So my Cid camped as though he were out in the wild hills.
sentences.n: montaña here is wild, uninhabited country generally, not just 'mountain'. f[o]sse — manuscript fuesse, Pidal's restored older form; commo si + subjunctive, exactly like modern como si fuese.

sentences.i: They have forbidden him all buying within the town of Burgos — of everything that counts as food; none would dare sell him so much as a penny's worth.
sentences.n: l'an = le an (le han): the letter's ban extends even to food. Burgos la casa — casa can mean the whole settlement: 'Burgos the town'. A dinarada is a dinero's-worth, the smallest purchase there is. The stage is set for the next tirada: Martín Antolínez will bring provisions out from Burgos at his own risk.
